name_original: Tratado de Tordesilhas / Tratado de Tordesillas
location_name: Tordesillas, Castile
main_actor: Crowns of Castile (Isabel I and Fernando II) and Portugal (João II), mediated by Pope Alexander VI
description: Signed on 7 June 1494, the treaty divided the newly contacted lands outside Europe between Castile and Portugal along a meridian 370 leagues west of the Cape Verde islands. Portugal received territory east of the line (eventually including Brazil), Castile everything to the west. The agreement was made without consultation of, or knowledge by, the tens of millions of Indigenous people whose lands and lives it partitioned. It is the documentary foundation of the Iberian colonial enterprise in the Americas.
ethical_notes: Treated as TREATY because it is a bilateral Iberian agreement, but the event's primary historical meaning is the legal-diplomatic authorization of what became a hemispheric conquest. Indigenous American polities were never notified and never consented. The Papal bulls Inter caetera (1493) form its juridical premise and are themselves a foundational document of colonial racism.

entity_links.notes: juridical foundation of the Spanish overseas empire

sources.citation: Davenport, F.G. (ed.) (1917). European Treaties bearing on the History of the United States and its Dependencies to 1648. Carnegie Institution, vol. I, pp. 84-100 (text of the treaty).
Parry, J.H. (1966). The Spanish Seaborne Empire. London: Hutchinson.

location_name: Valley of Mexico and central Mesoamerica
main_actor: Variola virus (smallpox) introduced by the Pánfilo de Narváez expedition, April 1520
description: A smallpox epidemic, introduced by an infected member of the Narváez relief force landing at Veracruz in April 1520, swept through the Valley of Mexico between September 1520 and the siege of Tenochtitlán in May-August 1521. Mexica sources (Florentine Codex Book XII) describe the sores covering bodies 'so that they could not move', mass deaths of adults during the siege and the killing of the emperor Cuitláhuac. The epidemic materially enabled the fall of Tenochtitlán by destroying defenders, command and the harvest of 1520. It was the first wave of an immunological catastrophe that reduced central Mexican population by an estimated 50-90 percent across the sixteenth century.
casualties_source: Acuna-Soto et al. (2002); Prem, H.J. (1991) estimate 40-50% of the Basin of Mexico population in the 1520 wave alone
ethical_notes: Recorded as EPIDEMIC but inseparable from CONQUEST: the European invasion introduced the pathogen, and the pathogen was indispensable to the conquest's success. Mexica narrators in the Florentine Codex (Book XII) describe the epidemic in their own voice; this is one of the best-documented Indigenous accounts of an early colonial disease catastrophe.

entity_links.notes: as vector of introduction via Narváez expedition

sources.citation: Sahagún, B. de (c. 1575-77). Historia General de las Cosas de Nueva España (Florentine Codex), Book XII (ed. Dibble & Anderson, University of Utah Press, 1975).
Prem, H.J. (1991). 'Disease outbreaks in Central Mexico during the Sixteenth Century', in N.D. Cook & W.G. Lovell (eds.), Secret Judgments of God. University of Oklahoma Press.
Acuna-Soto, R. et al. (2002). 'Megadrought and Megadeath in 16th Century Mexico'. Emerging Infectious Diseases, 8(4): 360-362.

location_name: México-Tenochtitlan, Basin of Mexico
main_actor: Hernán Cortés with Tlaxcalteca, Cempoaltec, Texcocan and other Mesoamerican allies against the Mexica under Cuauhtémoc
description: After the failed Noche Triste retreat (30 June 1520) and a reconsolidation at Tlaxcallan, a Castilian-Indigenous allied army besieged Tenochtitlán from 26 May 1521. On 13 August 1521 the city fell; the tlahtoani Cuauhtémoc was captured and later tortured and executed (1525). An estimated 100,000-240,000 Mexica died during the three-month siege from combat, starvation, and smallpox. The allied coalition was overwhelmingly Indigenous — perhaps 200,000 Tlaxcalteca, Texcocan and others — acting on their own political calculations against Mexica hegemony; the Spanish contingent numbered under 1,000.
casualties_source: Hassig, R. (2006). Mexico and the Spanish Conquest, 2nd ed.; siege estimates 100-240k Mexica deaths
ethical_notes: Classified CONQUEST per ETHICS-007 with explicit acknowledgment of the violence and the smallpox cascade (see the EPIDEMIC event 1520). The 'noble Cortés vs. barbaric Aztecs' narrative is rejected: the outcome was an Indigenous civil-strategic realignment in which Tlaxcalteca elites chose a Castilian alliance against Mexica imperial extraction, not a European triumph over a continent. Cuauhtémoc's subsequent torture (1525) is documented in Spanish sources and must not be omitted.

entity_links.notes: decisive Indigenous ally of the Castilian expedition

sources.citation: Sahagún, B. de (c. 1575-77). Florentine Codex, Book XII (Nahuatl-language Mexica account of the conquest).
Hassig, R. (2006). Mexico and the Spanish Conquest, 2nd ed. University of Oklahoma Press.
Restall, M. (2003). Seven Myths of the Spanish Conquest. Oxford University Press.
Townsend, C. (2019). Fifth Sun: A New History of the Aztecs. Oxford University Press.

name_original: Qhapaq Ataw Wallpaq hap'iyninkuna / Captura de Atahualpa
location_name: Cajamarca, Andean Peru
main_actor: Francisco Pizarro and the Castilian expedition against the Sapa Inka Atawallpa (Atahualpa)
description: Following the Cajamarca ambush of 16 November 1532 — during which Pizarro's force of ~168 men killed thousands of unarmed Inka retinue in a single afternoon and seized Atawallpa — the Sapa Inka was held hostage for eight months. A ransom filling one room with gold and two with silver (the so-called rescate) was paid and then the Castilians executed him anyway by garrote on 26 July 1533. Conquest of Qusqu followed in November 1533. The Inka defeat was enabled by an ongoing civil war between Atawallpa and Waskar, by endemic smallpox (which had killed the previous Sapa Inka Wayna Qhapaq ~1527), and by Castilian exploitation of anti-Inka grievances among Waskar's supporters and subject nations.
casualties_source: Hemming, J. (1970). The Conquest of the Incas (Cajamarca massacre ~2,000-7,000 unarmed retinue)
ethical_notes: Per ETHICS-007 the Cajamarca ambush is treated as part of a CONQUEST event but described with the violence intact: an unarmed retinue, a ransom paid, and then the execution anyway. No 'civilizing mission' framing. The execution of Atawallpa on the Catholic charge of idolatry and fratricide despite the paid ransom is foundational to the event's ethical meaning.

entity_links.notes: effective dissolution; a rump Neo-Inka state survives at Vilcabamba until 1572

sources.citation: Xerez, F. de (1534). Verdadera relación de la conquista del Perú. Seville. (eyewitness account by Pizarro's secretary).
Guaman Poma de Ayala, F. (c. 1615). Nueva crónica y buen gobierno. (Indigenous Andean account).
Hemming, J. (1970). The Conquest of the Incas. London: Macmillan.

location_name: Qusqu (Cuzco), Viceroyalty of Peru
main_actor: Viceroy Francisco de Toledo, executing the last Sapa Inka Tupaq Amaru I
description: On 24 September 1572, under Viceroy Toledo, Spanish forces captured the Neo-Inka capital of Vilcabamba and seized Tupaq Amaru I, the last reigning Sapa Inka of the rump Inka state that had persisted since 1537. After a summary trial on charges including treason and the deaths of Augustinian friars, he was publicly beheaded in the central plaza of Qusqu before a large Andean crowd who — according to the mestizo chronicler Titu Cusi — wailed as the blow fell. His execution terminated the formal Inka polity and initiated the Toledan reforms that entrenched the mita forced-labour system and the reducciones.
ethical_notes: Classified DISSOLUTION_STATE for the termination of the Neo-Inka state. The execution was a public political act carried out over objections from Cuzco's royal and ecclesiastical officials, several of whom appealed to Philip II; the king later reprimanded Toledo but did not punish him. The event is the symbolic terminus of the Inka civilisation as a self-governing political entity.

entity_links.notes: formal end of the Neo-Inka state at Vilcabamba

sources.citation: Titu Cusi Yupanqui (1570). Instrucción del Inca Don Diego de Castro Titu Cussi Yupangui. (contemporary Neo-Inka account).
Hemming, J. (1970). The Conquest of the Incas. London: Macmillan, chs. 20-21.
Mumford, J.R. (2012). Vertical Empire: The General Resettlement of Indians in the Colonial Andes. Duke University Press.

location_name: Tsenacommacah (Tidewater Virginia)
main_actor: Powhatan paramount chiefdom (Wahunsenacawh and Opechancanough) against the Virginia Company and Crown colony of Virginia
description: Three wars between the Powhatan confederation and the English settlers of Jamestown (founded 1607) spanned 1609-1614, 1622-1632 and 1644-1646. The 1622 uprising under Opechancanough killed roughly a quarter of the English settlers in a single day; the English response, framed as a war of extirpation, systematically burned Powhatan villages and destroyed corn stores over the following decade. The 1646 treaty formalised English sovereignty, reduced the Powhatan to tributary status and confined them to marginal reserved lands; by 1669 the English colonial census counted perhaps 2,000 surviving Powhatan from an estimated pre-contact population of 14,000-22,000.
casualties_source: Rountree, H. (1990). Pocahontas's People: The Powhatan Indians of Virginia Through Four Centuries
ethical_notes: The English colonial archive dominates; this entry follows Rountree's reconstruction centred on Powhatan agency. The English colonial use of scorched-earth tactics and deliberate destruction of food supplies is documented in Virginia Company records themselves (notably Governor Wyatt's orders, 1622).

entity_links.notes: Virginia, the first successful English colony on the mainland

sources.citation: Rountree, H.C. (1990). Pocahontas's People: The Powhatan Indians of Virginia Through Four Centuries. University of Oklahoma Press.
Gleach, F.W. (1997). Powhatan's World and Colonial Virginia: A Conflict of Cultures. University of Nebraska Press.

location_name: Wampanoag, Narragansett, and Nipmuc country (New England)
main_actor: Wampanoag sachem Metacom (Pometacom, known to the English as 'King Philip') and allied Narragansett and Nipmuc against the United Colonies of New England
description: From June 1675 a coalition of Wampanoag, Nipmuc, Narragansett and other New England Indigenous nations led by Metacom attacked more than half of the 90 English towns of New England in response to decades of land expropriation, religious coercion and the judicial execution of three Wampanoag men at Plymouth. At the Great Swamp Fight of 19 December 1675 the colonial militia massacred an estimated 300-1,000 Narragansett non-combatants, mostly women and children, in their winter encampment. Metacom was killed on 12 August 1676 at Mount Hope; his head was displayed on a pike at Plymouth for over two decades. Proportionally to population, this remains the bloodiest war ever fought on North American soil: around 800 English colonists and 3,000-5,000 Indigenous people died, and the military destruction of the New England nations opened the region to unimpeded English settlement.
casualties_source: Lepore, J. (1998). The Name of War: King Philip's War and the Origins of American Identity
ethical_notes: The colonial name 'King Philip's War' centres the English grievance; the Wampanoag name and leadership are restored here as the primary frame. The Great Swamp Fight of December 1675 meets the definition of MASSACRE under contemporary standards; English colonial records themselves acknowledge the fort contained mainly women, children and elders.

sources.citation: Lepore, J. (1998). The Name of War: King Philip's War and the Origins of American Identity. Alfred A. Knopf.
Drake, J.D. (1999). King Philip's War: Civil War in New England, 1675-1676. University of Massachusetts Press.
Church, B. (1716). Entertaining Passages Relating to Philip's War. (colonial primary).

location_name: Santa Fe de Nuevo México, northern Pueblo country
main_actor: Po'pay of Ohkay Owingeh and a coalition of Tewa, Tiwa, Towa, Keres and Zuni Pueblos, with Hopi participation
description: On 10 August 1680, Pueblo communities across the Rio Grande rose in coordinated rebellion planned by the Tewa religious leader Po'pay against Franciscan missionary repression, forced labour, and the droughts of the 1670s. Approximately 400 Spanish colonists and 21 of 33 Franciscan missionaries were killed; the surviving 2,000 colonists were expelled south to El Paso del Norte. Mission churches were burned, Catholic baptismal records destroyed, and Pueblo ceremonial life publicly restored. Independent Pueblo self-government was maintained for twelve years until the 1692 reconquest by Diego de Vargas; even after reconquest, the Pueblos retained considerably more religious and civic autonomy than they had enjoyed before 1680. It is the most successful Indigenous rebellion in the history of North America.
casualties_source: Knaut, A.L. (1995). The Pueblo Revolt of 1680
ethical_notes: Classified REVOLUTION to underline that the rebellion established an independent Pueblo political order for twelve years, not merely a violent incident. The name 'Pueblo Revolt' remains historically common; Po'pay's name is now inscribed on his statue in the US Capitol (contributed by New Mexico 2005).

sources.citation: Knaut, A.L. (1995). The Pueblo Revolt of 1680: Conquest and Resistance in Seventeenth-Century New Mexico. University of Oklahoma Press.
Weber, D.J. (ed.) (1999). What Caused the Pueblo Revolt of 1680? Bedford/St. Martin's.
Otermín, A. de (1681). Declaration of Pedro Naranjo of San Felipe (Spanish interrogation transcripts of Pueblo informants; in Hackett & Shelby, eds., 1942).

name_original: Jatun Ataw Tupaq Amaru II / Rebelión de Túpac Amaru II
location_name: Tinta, Qusqu and the southern Andean altiplano
main_actor: José Gabriel Condorcanqui (Tupaq Amaru II) and his wife Micaela Bastidas; the Aymara leader Tupaq Katari in Alto Perú (La Paz)
description: On 4 November 1780 the Andean kuraka Tupaq Amaru II executed the corregidor Antonio de Arriaga at Tinta, launching the largest Indigenous uprising in colonial Spanish America. The rebellion united Quechua and Aymara communities against the mita forced-labour draft, the reparto commercial monopoly, and fiscal reforms imposed by the Bourbon reforms of Charles III. At its peak the insurgency controlled a territory from Qusqu to Lake Titicaca; Tupaq Katari besieged La Paz twice in 1781. The movement was crushed by 1783. Tupaq Amaru II, his wife and children were publicly tortured and quartered at Qusqu on 18 May 1781; Tupaq Katari was quartered at Peñas on 15 November 1781. Reprisal executions killed an estimated 100,000 Andean people. The colonial administration forbade the Quechua language, the wearing of Inka dress, and public performance of Inka history.
casualties_source: O'Phelan Godoy, S. (1985). Rebellions and Revolts in Eighteenth-Century Peru
ethical_notes: Classified REVOLUTION because the insurgents articulated an explicit political programme of restored Inka sovereignty and the end of the mita. The colonial executions included deliberate cultural erasure: banning Quechua literary texts and Inka clothing is documented in the Royal Order of 21 April 1781. These bans are a formally declared silencing of Andean memory.
silence_reason: After 1781 the Spanish administration formally banned Quechua-language publications, performance of the Inka play Ollantay, and the wearing of Inka royal dress. These bans targeted the rebellion's cultural memory specifically and were partially respected until the 1810s.

sources.citation: O'Phelan Godoy, S. (1985). Rebellions and Revolts in Eighteenth-Century Peru and Upper Peru. Böhlau Verlag.
Stavig, W. (1999). The World of Tupac Amaru: Conflict, Community, and Identity in Colonial Peru. University of New Mexico Press.
Thomson, S. (2002). We Alone Will Rule: Native Andean Politics in the Age of Insurgency. University of Wisconsin Press.

name_original: Louisiana Purchase / Vente de la Louisiane
location_name: Paris; territory transferred centred on New Orleans
main_actor: First French Republic (Napoleon Bonaparte) and United States of America (Thomas Jefferson, Robert Livingston, James Monroe)
description: On 30 April 1803 the French First Consul Napoleon Bonaparte sold to the United States ~2,140,000 km² of North American territory — the Louisiana Territory — for 80 million francs (~$15 million), following the French strategic reversal in Saint-Domingue. The sale doubled the territory of the United States. Neither the Osage, Quapaw, Omaha, Ponca, Pawnee, Arikara, Mandan, Hidatsa, Lakota, Dakota, Cheyenne, Arapaho, Comanche, Caddo, Kiowa, Apache, Ute or dozens of other Indigenous nations inhabiting the territory was consulted, and the US subsequently treated the purchase as an implied right of eminent domain over their lands.
ethical_notes: Classified TREATY but documented with the crucial qualifier: the territory was inhabited by sovereign Indigenous nations who were neither consulted nor compensated. The entire subsequent 19th-century US continental expansion — Trail of Tears, Great Plains wars, California genocide — is juridically downstream of this sale between two European-descent states negotiating over someone else's homelands.

sources.citation: Treaty Between the United States of America and the French Republic (30 April 1803). (text in Miller's Treaties, vol. 2).
Kastor, P.J. (2004). The Nation's Crucible: The Louisiana Purchase and the Creation of America. Yale University Press.
Kukla, J. (2003). A Wilderness So Immense: The Louisiana Purchase and the Destiny of America. Alfred A. Knopf.

location_name: Dolores, Guanajuato, and throughout New Spain
main_actor: Miguel Hidalgo y Costilla; José María Morelos; Agustín de Iturbide (final settlement 1821)
description: On 16 September 1810 the parish priest Miguel Hidalgo rang the bells of Dolores and summoned the Indigenous and mestizo population of the Bajío to rise against the colonial administration, which had been delegitimised by the Napoleonic deposition of Ferdinand VII. After eleven years of war — during which Hidalgo (1811) and Morelos (1815) were captured and shot, and the radical programme of abolishing slavery and caste was sidelined — a conservative coalition under Iturbide achieved independence via the Plan de Iguala and Treaty of Córdoba (24 August 1821), producing the short-lived First Mexican Empire. The war killed an estimated 300,000-500,000 people, roughly 6 percent of New Spain's population, and left the new country economically wrecked.
casualties_source: Tutino, J. (1986). From Insurrection to Revolution in Mexico
ethical_notes: The Grito is conventionally celebrated as the founding of Mexico. Hidalgo's and Morelos's original programmes — abolition of slavery, abolition of the caste system, return of land to Indigenous communities — were quietly abandoned by the Iturbidist settlement of 1821, which preserved creole property and the Catholic Church. This gap between founding rhetoric and founding settlement is itself part of the event.

sources.citation: Tutino, J. (1986). From Insurrection to Revolution in Mexico: Social Bases of Agrarian Violence, 1750-1940. Princeton University Press.
Van Young, E. (2001). The Other Rebellion: Popular Violence, Ideology, and the Mexican Struggle for Independence, 1810-1821. Stanford University Press.
Rodríguez O., J.E. (1998). The Independence of Spanish America. Cambridge University Press.

location_name: Buenos Aires, Viceroyalty of the Río de la Plata
main_actor: Primera Junta de gobierno de Buenos Aires (Cornelio Saavedra, Mariano Moreno, Manuel Belgrano)
description: Between 18 and 25 May 1810 a series of open town-council meetings (cabildos abiertos) in Buenos Aires deposed Viceroy Cisneros and established the Primera Junta, nominally in the name of the captive Ferdinand VII but effectively an independent government of the Río de la Plata. The move was enabled by the British blockade of Spain and by the 1808 Napoleonic seizure of the peninsula. The event inaugurated a 15-year war of independence that would splinter the viceroyalty into modern Argentina, Uruguay, Paraguay and Bolivia; independence from Spain was formally declared at Tucumán in 1816.
ethical_notes: Paired with the Grito de Dolores as a foundational event of Spanish American independence. The Revolución de Mayo is often narrated as a liberal patriotic triumph; in practice the creole elites who made it had no programme for the Indigenous population of the interior or for the enslaved African-descended population of the coastal cities, which was not emancipated in Argentina until 1853.

entity_links.entity_name_original: Virreinato del Rio de la Plata
Provincias Unidas del Rio de la Plata

sources.citation: Chiaramonte, J.C. (1997). Ciudades, provincias, Estados: Orígenes de la Nación Argentina (1800-1846). Ariel.
Goldman, N. (ed.) (1998). Nueva Historia Argentina, tomo III: Revolución, república, confederación (1806-1852). Sudamericana.

location_name: Pampa de la Quinua, Ayacucho, Peru
main_actor: Antonio José de Sucre commanding the Gran Colombian and Peruvian patriot army against Viceroy José de La Serna
description: On 9 December 1824, Sucre's 5,780 patriot troops defeated the 6,900-strong royalist army of Viceroy La Serna at Ayacucho, capturing La Serna and ~2,000 of his men. The Capitulación de Ayacucho, signed that evening, ended Spanish continental rule in the Americas after 332 years. Upper Peru's independence followed within months as Bolivia (6 August 1825). Isolated royalist pockets held out at Callao until 1826 and Chiloé until 1826, but Ayacucho is the effective terminal event of the Spanish American mainland empire.
casualties_source: Lynch, J. (2006). Simón Bolívar: A Life
ethical_notes: Both armies were overwhelmingly composed of Indigenous and mestizo Andean soldiers conscripted by rival creole officer corps; the patriot-royalist split cut across, not along, ethnic lines. This is often obscured in nationalist narratives.

entity_links.notes: effective end of continental Spanish American empire

sources.citation: Sucre, A.J. de (1824). Parte oficial de la Batalla de Ayacucho. (primary).
Lynch, J. (2006). Simón Bolívar: A Life. Yale University Press.
Méndez, C. (2005). The Plebeian Republic: The Huanta Rebellion and the Making of the Peruvian State. Duke University Press.

name_original: Treaty of Guadalupe Hidalgo / Tratado de Guadalupe Hidalgo
location_name: Villa de Guadalupe Hidalgo, Valley of Mexico; battlefields from Texas to California
main_actor: United States of America (James K. Polk, Winfield Scott, Zachary Taylor) and the Mexican Republic (Antonio López de Santa Anna, Manuel de la Peña y Peña)
description: The US invaded Mexico following the disputed annexation of Texas (1845) and the Polk administration's expansionist programme articulated as Manifest Destiny. US forces occupied Mexico City in September 1847. The Treaty of Guadalupe Hidalgo, signed 2 February 1848, transferred ~55 percent of Mexican national territory — Alta California, Nuevo México, and territory in modern Arizona, Nevada, Utah, Colorado and Wyoming — to the US for $15 million and the assumption of $3.25 million in US citizens' claims. The treaty nominally guaranteed property rights of the approximately 100,000 Mexican nationals in the ceded territories (Article IX); these guarantees were systematically violated over the following decades through land adjudication commissions, squatter-friendly legislation and extralegal violence. Indigenous peoples of the ceded territory were not parties and received no recognition.
casualties_source: Henderson, T.J. (2007). A Glorious Defeat: Mexico and Its War with the United States (combat + disease, both sides)
ethical_notes: Classified TREATY per the formal settlement but the underlying event is a war of territorial conquest. The treaty's Article IX property guarantees for Mexican nationals in the ceded territories were violated in practice, a well-documented pattern. The Indigenous nations of the ceded land — Comanche, Navajo, Apache, Ute, Pueblo, Tongva, Chumash, Yokuts — were not parties and received no protection; the US proceeded to wage separate wars against each.

sources.citation: Treaty of Peace, Friendship, Limits, and Settlement between the United States of America and the Mexican Republic (2 February 1848).
Henderson, T.J. (2007). A Glorious Defeat: Mexico and Its War with the United States. Hill and Wang.
Greenberg, A. (2012). A Wicked War: Polk, Clay, Lincoln, and the 1846 US Invasion of Mexico. Alfred A. Knopf.

location_name: California, USA
main_actor: State of California, United States Army, state militia campaigns (Bald Hills War, Bloody Island Massacre 1850, Round Valley, Modoc War)
description: Between the US conquest of 1846 and the end of the Modoc War in 1873, the Indigenous population of California collapsed from an estimated 150,000 to about 30,000. The State of California and US Army conducted, sponsored, or tacitly permitted a sustained campaign of killings, forced labour, child abduction, and systematic destruction of food supplies. The California legislature appropriated at least $1.5 million between 1851 and 1859 to reimburse volunteer militias for hunting Indigenous people; Governor Peter Burnett declared in 1851 that 'a war of extermination will continue to be waged between the races, until the Indian race becomes extinct.' The 1850 'Act for the Government and Protection of Indians' legalised the kidnapping of Indigenous children as 'apprentices'. In 2019 California Governor Gavin Newsom formally apologised for the genocide.
casualties_source: Madley, B. (2016). An American Genocide: The United States and the California Indian Catastrophe, 1846-1873
silence_reason: For most of the twentieth century California textbooks and state public discourse denied the genocidal character of this period, describing the population collapse as 'decline', 'assimilation' or the consequence of 'disease and dislocation'. Madley's 2016 monograph, drawing on archival documentation of state funding for militia killings, ended plausible denial; formal state acknowledgment followed in 2019.
ethical_notes: Classified GENOCIDE per ETHICS-007 following Madley's decisive 2016 reconstruction, which established that the California state government knowingly financed a campaign meeting the Genocide Convention's definition. Euphemism ('decline', 'disappearance') is rejected.

sources.citation: Madley, B. (2016). An American Genocide: The United States and the California Indian Catastrophe, 1846-1873. Yale University Press.
Castillo, E.D. (1978). 'The Impact of Euro-American Exploration and Settlement', in Handbook of North American Indians, vol. 8: California. Smithsonian Institution.
Newsom, G. (2019). Executive Order N-15-19, Apology for the State's Historical Relationship with California Native Americans.

location_name: Sand Creek, Colorado Territory
main_actor: Colonel John Chivington and the 1st and 3rd Colorado Volunteer Cavalry Regiments
description: At dawn on 29 November 1864, the Colorado Volunteer Cavalry under Colonel Chivington attacked a sleeping Cheyenne and Arapaho encampment at Sand Creek — flying both the US flag and a white flag of truce by prior assurance from Major Wynkoop and Governor Evans. Approximately 230 Cheyenne and Arapaho were killed, at least two-thirds of them women, children and elders; the troops mutilated corpses and later publicly displayed body parts in Denver. The 1865 US congressional Joint Committee on the Conduct of the War concluded that the attack was a massacre of peaceful people; no participant was prosecuted.
casualties_source: Kelman, A. (2013). A Misplaced Massacre: Struggling over the Memory of Sand Creek
ethical_notes: Classified MASSACRE. The 1865 Congressional investigation (Report on the Conduct of the War) itself concluded that the attack was a massacre and recommended prosecution. Chivington was allowed to resign and faced no charges. The National Park Service in 2007 designated the site a National Historic Site, explicitly using the term 'massacre'.

sources.citation: US Congress, Joint Committee on the Conduct of the War (1865). 'Massacre of the Cheyenne Indians'.
Kelman, A. (2013). A Misplaced Massacre: Struggling over the Memory of Sand Creek. Harvard University Press.
Hoig, S. (1961). The Sand Creek Massacre. University of Oklahoma Press.

name_original: Guerra de la Triple Alianza / Guasu Ñorairõ
location_name: Paraguay, with operations into Brazil, Argentina and Uruguay
main_actor: Triple Alliance (Empire of Brazil, Argentina, Uruguay) against the Republic of Paraguay under Francisco Solano López
description: The longest and deadliest interstate war in Latin American history. Between 1864 and 1870 the Triple Alliance — Brazil, Argentina, and the Colorado government of Uruguay — fought Paraguay. Paraguay lost approximately 60-70 percent of its total population and at least 80-90 percent of adult men, from combat, forced conscription, disease and the Brazilian scorched-earth campaign in the final 1869-70 phase. Solano López was killed at Cerro Corá on 1 March 1870. Paraguay lost roughly 140,000 km² of territory and was reduced to a protectorate; its recovery took three generations. The war's demographic catastrophe meets multiple scholarly definitions of genocide, though it is conventionally classed as an interstate war.
casualties_source: Whigham, T.L. & Potthast, B. (1999). 'The Paraguayan Rosetta Stone'. Latin American Research Review 34(1) — 60-69% population loss
ethical_notes: Classified GENOCIDE because of the final-phase Brazilian scorched-earth campaign and the intentionality of destroying the Paraguayan male population; multiple recent scholars (including Whigham 2002, Doratioto 2002) argue this threshold was crossed. An alternative classification as interstate war (OTHER) would understate the demographic targeting. The figure is among the most contested in Latin American demographic history.

sources.citation: Whigham, T.L. (2002). The Paraguayan War, vol. 1: Causes and Early Conduct. University of Nebraska Press.
Whigham, T.L. & Potthast, B. (1999). 'The Paraguayan Rosetta Stone: New Insights into the Demographics of the Paraguayan War'. Latin American Research Review 34(1): 174-186.
Doratioto, F. (2002). Maldita Guerra: Nova História da Guerra do Paraguai. Companhia das Letras.

location_name: Isla Grande de Tierra del Fuego, Argentina and Chile
main_actor: Sheep estancia companies (Sociedad Explotadora de Tierra del Fuego, José Menéndez, Mauricio Braun), their hired 'Indian hunters' (Alexander McLennan, Sam Hyslop), and the Argentine and Chilean governments
description: Between the establishment of the first large-scale Patagonian sheep estancias in 1884 and ~1910 the Selk'nam (Ona) people of Tierra del Fuego were reduced from an estimated 3,000-4,000 to under 350 by a deliberate extermination campaign financed by the sheep companies, which paid bounties (reportedly £1 per pair of ears, later per head) to hired gunmen. Survivors were deported to Dawson Island missions where tuberculosis killed the majority. The last fluent Selk'nam speaker who learned the language as a mother tongue, Ángela Loij, died in 1974. The neighbouring Aónikenk and Yámana peoples suffered comparable collapses. The Selk'nam were officially declared extinct by the Argentine state; the modern Covadonga Ona community's recognition as an Indigenous people was granted by Argentina only in 2023.
casualties_source: Martinic, M. (2003). La Tierra de los Fuegos; Bascopé, J. (2008)
silence_reason: Throughout the twentieth century the Selk'nam genocide was absent from Argentine and Chilean national historiographies and school curricula. The 'extinction' of the Selk'nam was framed as a tragic loss of an archaic people rather than as a commercial extermination financed by named companies. Bascopé's 2008 archival work and the 2023 Argentine recognition are reversing this silence.
ethical_notes: Classified GENOCIDE per ETHICS-007 and ETHICS-008. The companies' bounty system is documented in estancia ledgers preserved at the Museo Regional de Magallanes in Punta Arenas. The 2023 Argentine recognition of the Selk'nam people reverses the state's prior 'extinct' classification and enables contemporary Selk'nam descendants' land and political claims.

sources.citation: Martinic, M. (2003). La Tierra de los Fuegos. Museo Regional de Magallanes.
Bascopé, J. (2008). En un area de tránsito: comerciantes, cazadores y trabajadores en la Patagonia austral (1874-1910). Santiago: Ocho Libros.
Chapman, A. (1982). Drama and Power in a Hunting Society: The Selk'nam of Tierra del Fuego. Cambridge University Press.

name_original: Atrocidades del Putumayo / Huitoto-Bora putumayo sufíriénai
location_name: Putumayo river basin, upper Amazon (Peru-Colombia frontier)
main_actor: Peruvian Amazon Company (Casa Arana) and its local overseers (Miguel Loaysa, Armando Normand), armed 'muchachos' of mixed indigenous auxiliaries, under Julio César Arana
description: Between approximately 1900 and the collapse of Andean rubber prices in 1912, the Peruvian Amazon Company — a London-listed extractive firm controlled by Julio César Arana — imposed a regime of forced labour, torture, sexual slavery and systematic murder on the Huitoto (Murui-Muina), Bora, Andoke, Ocaina, and Resígaro peoples of the Putumayo. An estimated 30,000-40,000 Indigenous people were killed or died from forced labour; the local population fell from ~50,000 pre-1900 to ~8,000-10,000 by 1912. The British consul Roger Casement's 1910-11 investigation, commissioned because the company was incorporated in London and employed Barbadian workers, exposed the scale internationally; the company collapsed commercially with the Asian rubber crash but no perpetrator was convicted in Peru.
casualties_source: Taussig, M. (1987). Shamanism, Colonialism, and the Wild Man; Casement, R. (1912). Blue Book on the Putumayo
silence_reason: The Putumayo atrocities were broadly known in 1912 but were subsequently written out of Peruvian and Colombian national historiographies. No Peruvian judicial accounting was completed; the principal perpetrator Arana served in the Peruvian Senate. The Huitoto and Bora survivors' testimonies were systematically discounted until the 1980s.
ethical_notes: Classified COLONIAL_VIOLENCE (near-GENOCIDE threshold for specific communities). Per ETHICS-008 the known_silence field is set because the official Peruvian record actively protected Arana and no reparation was ever provided to Indigenous descendants. Indigenous oral testimonies collected by Pineda (1985) and others are the primary sources for community-level detail.

entity_links.notes: via Casement investigation and London incorporation of the company

sources.citation: Casement, R. (1912). Correspondence respecting the treatment of British colonial subjects and native Indians employed in the collection of rubber in the Putumayo district (Cmd. 6266). HMSO (the 'Blue Book').
Taussig, M. (1987). Shamanism, Colonialism, and the Wild Man: A Study in Terror and Healing. University of Chicago Press.
Pineda Camacho, R. (2003). La Casa Arana en el Putumayo: El caucho y el proceso esclavista. Bogotá: Universidad Nacional de Colombia.

location_name: Mexico
main_actor: Francisco I. Madero, Emiliano Zapata, Francisco 'Pancho' Villa, Venustiano Carranza, Álvaro Obregón
description: A ten-year civil war opened by Madero's Plan de San Luis (5 October 1910) against the 35-year dictatorship of Porfirio Díaz. The war combined liberal-constitutional factions (Madero, Carranza), the Zapatista agrarian movement of Morelos (Plan de Ayala, 1911, demanding Tierra y Libertad), and Villa's Division of the North. Zapata was assassinated on 10 April 1919 by Carranza's government; Carranza was killed in May 1920; Villa was assassinated in 1923. The Revolution produced the 1917 Constitution with its radical Article 27 (land reform) and Article 123 (labour rights), though their implementation was deferred until the Cárdenas administration (1934-40). Estimated excess deaths 1.5-2.5 million, including 1918 flu mortality and war-driven emigration to the US.
casualties_source: Knight, A. (1986). The Mexican Revolution (2 vols); McCaa, R. (2003) 'Missing Millions'
ethical_notes: The Zapatista programme of communal land restitution for Indigenous and peasant villages (Plan de Ayala) was the most radical agrarian programme achieved by any early-20th-century revolution, though its implementation was partial. The 1910-20 period also saw large-scale displacement of Yaqui and Mayo Indigenous peoples from Sonora to Yucatán, which is itself a documented forced-deportation campaign begun under Díaz.

sources.citation: Knight, A. (1986). The Mexican Revolution, 2 vols. Cambridge University Press.
Womack, J. (1969). Zapata and the Mexican Revolution. Alfred A. Knopf.
McCaa, R. (2003). 'Missing Millions: The Human Cost of the Mexican Revolution'. Mexican Studies/Estudios Mexicanos 19(2): 367-400.

name_original: Okipasyon ameriken d Ayiti / US Occupation of Haiti
location_name: Haiti
main_actor: United States of America (Wilson and Coolidge administrations; US Marine Corps)
description: On 28 July 1915 President Woodrow Wilson ordered the US Marines to occupy Haiti following a coup. The occupation lasted nineteen years. The Marines disbanded the Haitian Assembly at gunpoint in 1917, rewrote the constitution (reportedly drafted by Franklin D. Roosevelt, then Assistant Secretary of the Navy) to permit foreign land ownership, imposed the corvée forced-labour system for road construction, and suppressed the peasant-led Caco resistance under Charlemagne Péralte and Benoît Batraville. An estimated 11,500-15,000 Haitians were killed in counterinsurgency operations; Péralte was killed on 1 November 1919 and his corpse photographed and circulated as a warning. Haitian customs revenues were controlled by the US until 1947. Parallel US occupations of the Dominican Republic (1916-1924) and Cuba (via the Platt Amendment 1903-1934) ran concurrently.
casualties_source: Renda, M. (2001). Taking Haiti: Military Occupation and the Culture of U.S. Imperialism, 1915-1940
ethical_notes: Classified COLONIAL_VIOLENCE. The occupation's economic programme — financial receivership, forced corvée labour, constitutional revision permitting foreign ownership — is the paradigm case for US early-20th-century interventions across the Caribbean ('Banana Wars'). The crucifixion-style photograph of Péralte was itself a political message understood at the time as a racially-coded warning.

sources.citation: Renda, M.A. (2001). Taking Haiti: Military Occupation and the Culture of U.S. Imperialism, 1915-1940. University of North Carolina Press.
Schmidt, H. (1971). The United States Occupation of Haiti, 1915-1934. Rutgers University Press.

location_name: Guatemala
main_actor: United States Central Intelligence Agency (Eisenhower, Dulles brothers), United Fruit Company, Colonel Carlos Castillo Armas
description: On 27 June 1954, following an eight-month CIA covert operation code-named PBSUCCESS, the democratically elected government of Jacobo Árbenz was forced to resign under the threat of US bombing and the invasion of a small CIA-trained force under Castillo Armas. Árbenz's principal offence was Decree 900 (1952), which expropriated uncultivated land from the United Fruit Company for redistribution to peasants with compensation at company-declared tax values. The coup installed a military junta, reversed the land reform, and began what became a 36-year civil war (1960-1996) in which an estimated 200,000 Guatemalans, overwhelmingly rural Maya, were killed. The CIA declassified the operation's records partially in 1997; US President Bill Clinton acknowledged US responsibility for 'significant human rights violations' in 1999.
casualties_source: Comisión para el Esclarecimiento Histórico (CEH), Guatemala memoria del silencio (1999)
ethical_notes: The immediate 1954 coup killed few people; its full casualty burden must be counted as the subsequent 36-year civil war, which the CEH concluded was directly enabled by the 1954 reversal of land reform and democratic institutions. The 'domino theory' and 'communist threat' framing that justified the coup is documented in CIA internal records as retrofitted to already-operational plans.

sources.citation: Comisión para el Esclarecimiento Histórico (1999). Guatemala: Memoria del Silencio. UN.
Cullather, N. (1999). Secret History: The CIA's Classified Account of its Operations in Guatemala, 1952-1954. Stanford University Press.
Gleijeses, P. (1991). Shattered Hope: The Guatemalan Revolution and the United States, 1944-1954. Princeton University Press.

location_name: Cuba
main_actor: Movimiento 26 de Julio (Fidel Castro, Raúl Castro, Ernesto 'Che' Guevara, Camilo Cienfuegos, Celia Sánchez, Haydée Santamaría)
description: From the failed 26 July 1953 attack on the Moncada barracks to the entry of the rebel army into Havana on 8 January 1959, the Movimiento 26 de Julio overthrew the US-backed dictatorship of Fulgencio Batista. The new government expropriated US corporate holdings (US-owned sugar, telephone, electricity, oil companies), declared the revolution socialist in 1961, and survived the April 1961 CIA-organised Bay of Pigs invasion. The October 1962 Cuban Missile Crisis followed. The US economic embargo imposed in 1960-62 remained in force into the 2020s; hundreds of thousands of Cubans emigrated to the US. The Revolution transformed Cuba's political-economic structure and remains the longest-surviving self-declared socialist state in the Americas.
casualties_source: Pérez-Stable, M. (2012). The Cuban Revolution (war of 1956-58: ~2,000-3,000; post-1959 revolutionary trials ~3,000 executions per scholarly consensus)
ethical_notes: The Revolution's social achievements (literacy, healthcare, infant mortality reduction) and its repression of dissent (political prisoners, suppression of LGBT people under the UMAP camps 1965-68, emigration restrictions) are both part of its historical record and both documented here in principle. Cuban and Cuban-exile historiographies remain sharply divided on casualty estimates, especially for the immediate post-1959 revolutionary tribunals.

sources.citation: Pérez-Stable, M. (2012). The Cuban Revolution: Origins, Course, and Legacy, 3rd ed. Oxford University Press.
Guerra, L. (2012). Visions of Power in Cuba: Revolution, Redemption, and Resistance, 1959-1971. University of North Carolina Press.

name_original: Golpe de Estado en Chile / Wingka trafkintu Txipanga
location_name: Chile
main_actor: Chilean armed forces under General Augusto Pinochet, supported by the US Nixon administration and CIA; targeting the Unidad Popular government of Salvador Allende
description: On 11 September 1973 the Chilean armed forces under Pinochet, with documented US support (including funding of anti-Allende strikes and the CIA's Track II programme), bombed the presidential palace La Moneda in Santiago. President Allende died during the attack. The 17-year military dictatorship that followed killed or disappeared at least 3,216 people (Rettig and Valech commissions' confirmed cases), tortured an estimated 38,000, exiled ~200,000, and imposed radical neoliberal economic restructuring (the 'Chicago Boys' programme). Pinochet was arrested in London on a Spanish warrant in 1998 but died in 2006 without criminal conviction. Documented CIA and State Department files (released 1999-2000) confirm the Nixon administration's direct encouragement of the coup.
casualties_source: Informe Rettig (1991) and Informe Valech (2004-11), Chilean state truth commissions
ethical_notes: Classified COLONIAL_VIOLENCE because of the external sponsorship (US) and the state-terrorist character of the regime. A separate GENOCIDE classification for Mapuche-targeted repression in specific regions has been argued by Chilean legal scholars but is not consensus. The National Stadium used as a detention/torture centre in the first weeks is one of the best-documented such facilities of the Cold War.

sources.citation: Comisión Nacional de Verdad y Reconciliación (1991). Informe Rettig. Santiago.
Kornbluh, P. (2003). The Pinochet File: A Declassified Dossier on Atrocity and Accountability. The New Press.
Stern, S.J. (2006). Battling for Hearts and Minds: Memory Struggles in Pinochet's Chile, 1973-1988. Duke University Press.

location_name: Argentina, Chile, Uruguay, Paraguay, Bolivia, Brazil; operations in US, Italy, France
main_actor: Intelligence services of Argentina (SIDE), Chile (DINA), Uruguay, Paraguay, Bolivia, Brazil; United States CIA as facilitator
description: Operation Condor was a formalised transnational counterinsurgency network agreed in Santiago in November 1975 between the intelligence services of six South American military regimes. Its three phases coordinated information exchange, cross-border kidnapping and assassination of political opponents (notably the 1976 Washington car-bomb murder of Orlando Letelier), and external operations reaching Europe. Estimated cross-border 'Condor' victims range 402-672, including Chilean, Uruguayan, Bolivian, Paraguayan and Argentine exiles disappeared in third countries. The operation was exposed by the accidental 1992 discovery of the 'Archives of Terror' in Asunción, Paraguay (~700,000 documents). Argentine courts convicted Argentine and Uruguayan officers for Condor participation in 2016.
casualties_source: McSherry, J.P. (2005). Predatory States; Archives of Terror, Paraguay
silence_reason: The full extent of Condor remained secret from 1975 until the 1992 discovery of the Archives of Terror. Specific cross-border disappearances were long attributed to 'common crime' or denied outright by the intelligence services involved. US declassification of State Department cables (2000-2015) continues to fill archival gaps.
ethical_notes: Classified COLONIAL_VIOLENCE given the US CIA facilitation role and the state-terror character of the operation across borders. Per ETHICS-008, the long institutional silence is recorded; several specific victim cases remain 'disappeared' pending archival confirmation.

entity_links.entity_name_original: Provincias Unidas del Rio de la Plata

sources.citation: McSherry, J.P. (2005). Predatory States: Operation Condor and Covert War in Latin America. Rowman & Littlefield.
Dinges, J. (2004). The Condor Years: How Pinochet and His Allies Brought Terrorism to Three Continents. The New Press.
Archivo del Terror (1992). Centro de Documentación y Archivo para la Defensa de los Derechos Humanos, Asunción.

name_original: Dictadura cívico-militar argentina / Proceso de Reorganización Nacional
location_name: Argentina
main_actor: Argentine armed forces junta under Jorge Rafael Videla, Emilio Massera, Orlando Agosti, Roberto Viola, Leopoldo Galtieri, Reynaldo Bignone
description: On 24 March 1976 a military junta deposed President Isabel Perón and initiated the 'Proceso de Reorganización Nacional'. Over seven years approximately 30,000 people — the figure endorsed by successor Argentine truth commissions and by the courts — were 'disappeared' (detained, tortured and killed without acknowledgment) in a network of ~500 clandestine detention centres (notably ESMA in Buenos Aires). Systematic stealing of ~500 children born in captivity to detained women, who were then killed, was documented by the Abuelas de Plaza de Mayo and has led to ~130 identifications to date (2023). The 1984 CONADEP report (Nunca Más) established the official record; the 1985 Juicio a las Juntas tried and convicted the commanders; subsequent amnesty laws (1986-87) were nullified in 2005, and trials of hundreds of perpetrators continue. Multiple Argentine federal courts have ruled that the Proceso was a genocide under the Genocide Convention's definition.
casualties_source: CONADEP (1984) Nunca Más (verified 8,961 cases; successor scholarship and Argentine court rulings accept ~30,000)
ethical_notes: Classified GENOCIDE per ETHICS-007 consistent with multiple Argentine federal court rulings (notably the 2006 conviction of Etchecolatz where the court wrote that the crimes were 'committed in the context of the genocide that took place in the Republic of Argentina'). The political-group targeting basis distinguishes this from some narrower international definitions; Argentine jurisprudence has accepted genocide as a legal classification.

entity_links.entity_name_original: Provincias Unidas del Rio de la Plata

sources.citation: Comisión Nacional sobre la Desaparición de Personas (CONADEP) (1984). Nunca Más: Informe de la CONADEP. EUDEBA.
Feierstein, D. (2007). El genocidio como práctica social: entre el nazismo y la experiencia argentina. Fondo de Cultura Económica.
Robben, A.C.G.M. (2005). Political Violence and Trauma in Argentina. University of Pennsylvania Press.
